insights.finding: Agency producers manually populate 311-399+ line items in Excel-based AICP bid forms, causing errors and formula breakage
evidence: AICP bid form contains 311-399+ line items across sections A through X
Hot Budget (industry standard) is a single-user Excel macro workbook — Mac only
Line producers start with crew rates (Section B), then build out equipment, locations, art dept, talent, post
Manual entry across 300+ items is error-prone with frequent formula breakage
No single tool covers create-manage-compare-review-track across the full workflow

insights.finding: Version chaos: bid revisions circulate as email attachments with no structured change tracking
evidence: Multiple bid revisions emailed as file attachments (v1, v2, v2_final, v2_final_FINAL)
No built-in change tracking — changes between bid versions require manual comparison
Client/agency may not see what changed between revisions
Hot Budget is single-user desktop-only; no real-time collaboration
EP and line producer cannot work simultaneously on the same bid

insights.finding: Triple bid comparison is entirely manual — agency producers compare 3+ bids section-by-section in separate Excel files
evidence: Agency producer must manually compare 3+ bid forms section-by-section
No standardized comparison tool — done in spreadsheets or side-by-side printouts
Identifying cost discrepancies across bids requires line-by-line inspection
BriefBid manages the RFP process but does not provide line-item comparison
Time-consuming, inconsistent comparison criteria across reviewers

insights.finding: Navigating 300+ line items across sections A-X creates severe cognitive overload for producers
evidence: Sections A through X span pre-production, crew, equipment, locations, talent, post-production, and custom items
Scrolling through a single Excel workbook with multiple tabs makes it easy to miss sections
Topsheet (page 1) rolls up category totals but detail lives deep in section tabs
Recent expansion of post-production sections Q-W added complexity
Producers maintain separate client-facing (topsheet) and internal (full breakdown) views

insights.finding: SAG-AFTRA and IATSE rate lookups are manual — producers reference external sources for talent and crew rates
evidence: SAG-AFTRA rates, IATSE rates, and equipment rental rates must be manually referenced
Annual wage bumps built into IATSE-AICP agreements — not a single flat rate
Different rates by region and IATSE local (665, 798, 399, etc.)
2024 Low Budget Digital Waiver changed session and overtime rate negotiability
Outdated rates and calculation errors are common when referencing externally

insights.finding: Markup calculations (25% production fee, 3% insurance, 15% travel handling) are manually applied and error-prone
evidence: Standard production company markup: 25% applied to below-the-line subtotal
Insurance: 3% on Sections A-K production costs
Travel handling fee: 15% minimum when travel requirements are simple
Above-the-line vs below-the-line distinction affects which markups apply
Math errors in high-stakes financial documents can cost jobs

insights.finding: No single tool covers the end-to-end workflow: create bid, manage triple bid, compare, review, track actuals
evidence: Bid creation: Hot Budget / Saturation / GetActual
Triple bid management: BriefBid or manual email
Bid comparison: manual in Excel — no dedicated tool
Client review/approval: email/PDF or meeting
Actuals tracking: Wrapbook / separate system
Version control: manual file naming conventions

insights.finding: 88% of spreadsheets contain at least one formula error of 1%+ magnitude
evidence: Ray Panko (U. Hawaii) foundational research on spreadsheet error rates
94% of business spreadsheets have critical errors per 2024 Frontiers of Computer Science study
2-5% of all formula cells contain errors even for experienced users
With 311-399+ line items and markup dependencies, probability of at least one material error per bid approaches certainty

insights.finding: Cloud-native tools (Saturation, GetActual) are emerging but Hot Budget remains the industry standard
evidence: Hot Budget: Excel macro, single-user, Mac-only, ~$199 — dominant for AICP topsheets
Saturation: cloud-native, real-time collaboration, Wrapbook integration
GetActual: live multi-party budgets, bid package PDF export
BlinkBid: cloud-based with AICP/AICE pre-populated catalogs, e-signatures
Cloud migration underway but slow — familiarity with Excel keeps Hot Budget dominant

insights.finding: Holding companies (WPP, Publicis, Omnicom) are centralizing production, creating in-house vs. independent tensions
evidence: WPP Production — single global platform combining Hogarth with group production teams
Publicis operating as one company for a decade with Prodigious in-house production
Omnicom Production mirrors WPP's consolidation model
In-house production sometimes competes in triple bids against independents — conflict of interest
Centralized rate databases and procurement teams are competitive differentiators

insights.finding: AI-driven production promises 30-60% cost savings, creating new budget line items and estimation challenges
evidence: AI production cost savings: 30-60% vs conventional methods
Projects that took 6+ months now completed in days with AI tools
AI-Video market growing from $3.86B (2024) to $42.29B (2033) — 32.2% CAGR
Companies increasing AI spending by nearly 40%
No standard AICP line items for AI-specific costs yet

insights.finding: Bid win rates average 17-25% — production companies invest significant time in losing bids
evidence: Average win rates: 17-25% (4-6:1 bid ratio)
RFP-to-award timeline: 10-16 weeks total
Typical bid turnaround: 3 working days for initial compilation
Production companies sink significant time and money into bid prep with no guarantee
Private work target: 4:1 bid ratio (25%+ win rate)

insights.finding: Cost estimation relies on producer experience — no systematic rate databases lead to inconsistent pricing
evidence: Institutional knowledge is the primary source for cost estimation
Junior producers struggle without experienced mentorship for rate benchmarks
No industry-wide published benchmarks for estimation error rates
Regional cost variations have limited comparative data available
Smart defaults from rate databases would eliminate guesswork

insights.finding: The AICP form is a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of financial calculations — changing shoot days cascades to crew, equipment, location, catering, and travel
evidence: The form is NOT just a form — it is simultaneously a rules engine, a calculation graph, and a reporting tool
OT calculations (1.5x after 8hrs, 2x after 12hrs), union rate cards, markup rules are embedded
Changing one variable cascades to multiple dependent sections
Dependencies are implicit in the current Excel-based tools
No tool provides impact preview before committing changes

insights.finding: Users are forced to be the computation engine — performing mental simulations, manual dependency tracking, and memory-based navigation
evidence: The real problem is not 'the form is long' — the system does not externalize complexity
Users hold budget totals, category breakdowns, and dependency chains simultaneously
No search or filter across 300+ rows
No persistent context — users lose position constantly across tabs
No diff tracking, change logs, or dependency mapping

insights.finding: Each AICP bid costs $10,000-$12,000+ in labor and hard costs — at 17-25% win rates, $50-72K is spent per won job
evidence: Each bid: $10-12K in hard costs and producer labor
6 bids x $10-12K = $50-72K total investment per won project
Mid-level agency producer ($51/hr) spends 24 hours on single bid = $1,224 labor
Triple bid agency-side labor: $3,672-$5,508 per job
Mid-sized agency (50-100 bids/year): $183K-$551K wasted annually in producer time

insights.finding: Total quantifiable waste for a 200-bid agency: $1.07M-$1.6M/year in producer time, rework, and revision cycles
evidence: Producer labor per bid: $1,224 (24hrs at $51/hr)
Revision overhead: 2-3 rounds per bid
Triple bid comparison labor: $3,672-$5,508 per job
Error correction and rework time
Cancelled bid losses: $10-12K+ per cancellation

insights.finding: DOJ subpoenaed WPP, Publicis, Omnicom, and IPG in 2016-2017 for alleged bid rigging — cleared in 2018 but systemic concerns remain
evidence: DOJ issued civil investigative demands to holding company subsidiaries
Companies cleared in 2018 but the investigation highlighted systemic triple-bid fairness concerns
In-house production arms compete against independent production companies with information advantage
Agency is simultaneously competitor and judge in triple bid process
No transparency into whether other bidders are agency-owned entities

insights.finding: Bids get cancelled because the agency never sold the job to the client — $10-12K+ loss per cancellation with zero compensation
evidence: Production companies have no visibility into whether the job is real
No standard compensation for bid preparation time on cancelled projects
Information asymmetry: prod companies don't know who else is bidding or if job is real
Free pitch labor: treatments + creative approaches with no compensation for losers

insights.finding: The AICP form expanded from sections A-N to A-X, with Q-W incorporating AICE post-production and social deliverables
evidence: Original form had sections A through N
Post-production sections Q through W added for AICE integration
Social media deliverables introduced new category-specific line items
Expansion increased complexity without corresponding UX improvements
Section navigation became more complex with the expanded form

insights.finding: 94% of business spreadsheets have critical errors per 2024 Frontiers of Computer Science study by Prof. Pak-Lok Poon
evidence: 94% error rate across business spreadsheets
2-5% of all formula cells contain errors even with experienced users
Errors compound across dependent calculations in AICP markup chain
Financial impact is amplified by the high-stakes nature of production budgets

insights.finding: Hot Budget is Mac-only with no cloud collaboration, locking out Windows users and distributed teams entirely
evidence: Hot Budget runs as an Excel macro workbook — Mac only
No cloud sync, no real-time collaboration
Read-only bug triggered by slash characters in filenames
No version history beyond manual file saving
Excel 365 compatibility issues documented

insights.finding: ABID — AICP's own digital bid management platform — launched October 2020 and went offline December 15, 2024
evidence: AICP launched ABID as official digital bidding platform
Platform ran for 4 years before shutdown
Industry's own attempt at modernizing bid workflow failed
No official replacement announced
Demonstrates difficulty of solving this workflow at industry level

insights.finding: Changing usage rights from 'Digital US 13 weeks' to 'All Media Global 1 Year' can 10x talent costs — the form does not surface this dependency
evidence: Usage/Rights is the largest cost variable after crew
Session fees, buyout multipliers, and usage fees all cascade from usage selection
Territory expansion (US to Global) multiplies base rates
Duration expansion (13 weeks to 1 year) further multiplies
No warning or impact preview when changing usage fields

insights.finding: AD overtime begins at hour 12, not hour 10 like the rest of the crew — an easy-to-miss rule and documented source of errors
evidence: AICP rules specify different OT thresholds by department
Standard crew OT: 1.5x after 10 hours, 2x after 12 hours
AD department OT: starts at 12th hour, not 10th
This exception is frequently missed in manual bid construction
Formula errors from applying wrong OT threshold are common

insights.finding: SAG-AFTRA 2024 Low Budget Digital Waiver unified connected and unconnected shoot rates, removing free negotiation on session and overtime
evidence: 2024 waiver restructured the Low Budget Digital rate card
Connected and unconnected shoot rates unified into single structure
Free negotiation on session and OT rates removed
MPU extended to 1 year from first use or 13 weeks after last production date
Producers must now use fixed rate tables instead of negotiated rates

insights.finding: IATSE-AICP agreement restructured to annual wage bumps, requiring dynamic budget escalation modeling
evidence: Not a flat contract rate — annual escalators built in
Different rates by IATSE local (665, 798, 399, etc.)
Producers must reference correct year and region for accurate rates
No automated rate lookup in current bidding tools
Manual reference to external PDFs and websites for rate sheets

insights.finding: The US video/film production market is valued at $40.9 billion in 2025, growing at 2% YoY
evidence: IBISWorld / Simulmedia market size data
Steady growth despite economic headwinds
National commercial production tier: $50K-$500K+ per project
Local: $3K-$15K, Regional: $15K-$50K
Crew per day: $10K-$100K+; Equipment: $5K-$50K

insights.finding: AI-Video market growing from $3.86B (2024) to $42.29B (2033) at 32.2% CAGR — fastest-growing segment in production
evidence: Runway, Sora, Veo driving AI-video adoption
Virtual production hardware costs dropped 40% since 2022
Projects that took 6+ months now completed in days
No standard AICP line items for AI-specific costs
New budget categories emerging: tokens, compute, AI tool licensing

insights.finding: Virtual production hardware costs dropped 40% since 2022, making LED volume stages viable for mid-budget productions
evidence: LED volume stages previously only viable for tentpole films
40% hardware cost reduction opens mid-budget commercial market
New budget line items needed for virtual production
Mixed shoot approaches (practical + virtual) create estimation complexity
Equipment rental vs. purchase decisions shift cost modeling

insights.finding: Companies increasing AI budget spending by nearly 40%, with cost allocations shifting toward AI tools across pre- and post-production
evidence: Budget allocations shifting from traditional to AI-augmented workflows
Pre-production: AI for storyboarding, location scouting, casting
Post-production: AI for VFX, color grading, sound design
Cost savings not yet standardized in AICP form structure
No industry consensus on how to budget AI tool costs

insights.finding: Progressive disclosure is the critical UX pattern: show section summaries first, expand to individual line items on demand
evidence: NNGroup research supports progressive disclosure for complex data
Section A-X summaries as primary view
Expand to individual line items on demand
Reduces cognitive load from 300+ rows to manageable sections
Topsheet concept already exists — needs better digital implementation

insights.finding: Inline editing with keyboard navigation (Tab/Enter/Escape) is the baseline UX requirement for data-dense financial tools
evidence: Power users (producers) expect spreadsheet-like keyboard navigation
Tab between cells, Enter to confirm, Escape to cancel
Double-click to edit cell content
Arrow keys for navigation between rows and columns
Reference products: Airtable, Linear, Notion all implement this pattern

insights.finding: Side-by-side bid comparison across all 311+ line items with variance highlighting is the highest-priority missing feature
evidence: Agency producers currently compare 3+ bids manually in separate Excel files
No tool provides line-item level comparison across bids
Variance highlighting would surface cost discrepancies instantly
Cost consultants need this for audit and compliance review
Currently takes 4-6 hours per comparison round

insights.finding: Version history with branching is the direct replacement for 'final-v3-ACTUAL.xlsx' naming conventions
evidence: Git-like revision tracking for bid versions
Branch for client-specific adjustments
Merge changes back to master bid
Diff view between versions with change summaries
Auto-generated revision notes: '+$12K due to crew increase'

insights.finding: Impact preview before committing changes is the core dependency-awareness feature: 'changing shoot days will affect X items by $Y'
evidence: Users need to see cascade effects before making changes
Shoot days affect crew, equipment, locations, catering, travel
Currently no way to preview impact without manual calculation
Would eliminate 'surprise' budget changes that break targets
Reference: Figma's constraint preview before resize

insights.finding: Role presets with current union rates (DP $3,500/day, Gaffer $1,800/day, PA $250/day) would eliminate the most repetitive manual entry
evidence: Most common crew roles are repeated across every bid
Rate lookup is manual — checking external PDFs and websites
Presets with region-specific rates would save hours per bid
Annual rate escalators could be built into presets
Template system from previous bids would reduce cold-start time

insights.finding: WCAG 2.1 AA requires role='grid' for interactive data grids, with roving tabindex and aria-sort on sortable columns
evidence: role='grid' not role='table' for interactive financial grids
Roving tabindex for cell navigation
aria-sort on sortable columns, aria-colcount/aria-rowcount for virtualized grids
4.5:1 contrast ratio for normal text
Color must not be sole conveyor of over/under budget state

insights.finding: Undo support for all financial edits is required under WCAG 2.1 AA criterion 3.3.4 (Error Prevention)
evidence: WCAG 3.3.4: Error Prevention for legal, financial, or data transactions
All financial edits must be reversible
Multi-level undo for complex editing sessions
Undo history should survive session refresh
Currently no undo in Excel-based AICP tools beyond Ctrl+Z

insights.finding: Showbiz Budgeting 10 requires $399 license — its complexity is designed for production accountants, not commercial producers
evidence: $399 base license with cloud as additional subscription
Steep learning curve designed for film/TV production accounting
Per-machine licensing model
Not optimized for AICP commercial production workflow
Cloud collaboration is an add-on, not built-in

insights.finding: The RFP-to-award pipeline spans 10-16 weeks — a timeline mismatch with the 3-day bid turnaround expectation
evidence: 5 stages: planning, RFP development, distribution, evaluation, selection
10-16 weeks total cycle
Initial bid turnaround: 3 working days expected
Multiple revision rounds within the cycle
Timeline pressure creates quality tradeoffs in bid accuracy

insights.finding: Straightforward project bids come within 5-10% of each other, meaning weeks of triple-bid overhead yields marginal price differentiation
evidence: Competitive bids converge on similar pricing for standard projects
Weeks of comparison effort for <10% price spread
Agency overhead for managing triple bid process is significant
Cost consultants further narrow the price band
Value differentiation shifts to creative treatment, not price

insights.finding: Production companies must bid against agency-owned production units where the agency is simultaneously competitor and judge
evidence: In-house production arms of holding companies compete in triple bids
Agency has access to competing bids while also submitting their own
No transparency into whether other bidders are agency-owned
Information asymmetry disadvantages independent production companies
Cost savings from in-house may not reflect true market pricing

insights.finding: Cost consultants are 'perpetual pain' — hidden markups get buried in line items to survive audit scrutiny
evidence: LBBOnline describes cost consultants as 'perpetual pain' for production companies
Markups get restructured across line items to avoid flagging
15% variance threshold triggers line-by-line audit
Production companies develop strategies to distribute markup
Creates adversarial dynamic between producers and auditors

insights.finding: DEI budget line items are emerging as standard — Fortune 1000 average is $1.5M/year (range $30K-$5.1M)
evidence: DEI line items becoming expected in production budgets
Fortune 1000 average: $1.5M/year, median: $1.2M
Wide range: $30K to $5.1M depending on company size
No standardized AICP form field for DEI costs
Budget allocated across crew hiring, vendor selection, accessibility

insights.finding: Corporate Transparency Act (Jan 1, 2024) requires 32M+ US businesses including production companies to report beneficial ownership
evidence: Effective January 1, 2024
32M+ US businesses affected
Production companies must disclose beneficial ownership to FinCEN
Additional compliance burden for small production companies
May affect how shell entities and LLCs are used in production

insights.finding: Airtable, Linear, Figma, and Stripe Dashboard are primary reference products for bidding tool UX
evidence: Airtable: linked records, database views, relational data
Linear: keyboard-first, command palette, real-time updates
Figma: real-time multiplayer, shared cursors, version history
Stripe Dashboard: financial hierarchy, drill-down from totals to transactions
All four combine data density with progressive disclosure

insights.finding: Five core jobs-to-be-done: Construct fast, Understand where money goes, Adjust and see impact, Defend costs, Iterate across versions
evidence: Construct: add line items quickly, reuse previous bids, stay within targets
Understand: where money is going, which sections are heavy, what drives cost
Adjust: change assumptions, see impact instantly across dependent calculations
Defend: explain costs clearly to stakeholders with data-backed justification
Iterate: multiple revisions, compare versions, maintain accuracy through changes

insights.finding: The global advertisement production services market is $1.93B (2024), projected to reach $3.66B by 2033 at 7.4% CAGR
evidence: Market Growth Reports data
7.4% compound annual growth rate
Growth driven by digital content demand and multi-platform deliverables
Social media deliverables expanding the total addressable market
Regional growth varies — US and Asia-Pacific leading

insights.finding: The AICP form's 4-level hierarchy (Section > Category > Line Item > Calculation) demands a tree-based UI with expand/collapse at every level
evidence: L1: Sections (A-X) — macro budget buckets
L2: Categories — crew, talent, equipment, locations, travel within sections
L3: Line Items — individual cost entries (e.g., DP @ $3,500/day x 3 days)
L4: Calculations — subtotals, markups, contingency, grand total
Tree-based navigation enables drill-down without losing context

themes.name: pain-point
themes.description: Core user frustrations with existing bidding tools, processes, and manual workflows

themes.name: ai-impact
themes.description: AI is compressing VFX/post costs by 28-40%, creating new budget categories and rendering traditional estimation frameworks obsolete

themes.name: competitive-landscape
themes.description: Seven+ tools with zero end-to-end coverage — ABID failure, Saturation/BlinkBid cloud migration, missing intelligence layers

themes.name: bid-creation
themes.description: Pain points in creating AICP bid forms — manual entry, complex structure, formula errors across 300+ line items

themes.name: bid-economics
themes.description: Win rates, cost per bid, waste quantification, and the economics of the triple bid process

themes.name: cognitive-load
themes.description: Users forced to be the computation engine — mental simulations, dependency tracking, memory-based navigation

themes.name: collaboration
themes.description: Lack of real-time collaboration, version control, and structured revision tracking in bid workflows

themes.name: form-structure
themes.description: AICP sections A-X, 4-level hierarchy, topsheet, above-the-line vs below-the-line distinctions

themes.name: triple-bid-process
themes.description: Systemic issues with the triple bid requirement — DOJ investigation, agency self-dealing, information asymmetry

themes.name: markup-complexity
themes.description: 25% production fee, 3% insurance, 15% travel handling, fringe loading, overtime rules, and usage/rights cascades

themes.name: union-compliance
themes.description: SAG-AFTRA, IATSE, DGA rate changes — non-overlapping calendars, AI frameworks, annual wage bumps

themes.name: market-context
themes.description: $40.9B US market, AI savings, virtual production trends, global growth, DEI budgets

themes.name: dependency-awareness
themes.description: Shoot day cascade effects, hidden dependency chains, usage/rights cost multipliers

themes.name: ux-patterns
themes.description: Progressive disclosure, inline editing, data grid design, keyboard-first, reference products

themes.name: feature-request
themes.description: High-leverage product opportunities: hierarchy, dependency mapping, version diff, impact preview, rate presets

themes.name: regulatory
themes.description: CTA 2024, ABID shutdown, DOJ investigation, bid rigging history, SAG-AFTRA/IATSE contract changes

themes.name: tool-limitations
themes.description: Hot Budget Mac-only lock-in, Showbiz complexity, ABID shutdown, Excel fragility, no cloud collaboration

personas.name: Sarah Chen — Agency Producer
personas.role: Senior Agency Producer, Publicis Groupe — 8 years in broadcast production. Manages $2-5M annual bid volume across 15-20 projects.
personas.quote: I spend more time formatting Excel cells than actually evaluating creative treatments.
goals: Create accurate AICP bids quickly under tight 3-day deadlines
Compare triple bids efficiently and present clear recommendations to client
Keep bid revisions organized across multiple stakeholders
Ensure SAG-AFTRA/IATSE compliance without manual rate lookups
painPoints: Comparing 3+ bids section-by-section in separate Excel files takes 4-6 hours
No real-time collaboration — EP and line producer can't work simultaneously
Version chaos: v1, v2, v2_final, v2_final_FINAL across email threads
Client asks 'what changed?' between revisions with no diff tool available
behaviors: Distributes bid specs to 3+ production companies simultaneously
Reviews topsheet summaries first, then dives into section detail
Uses Hot Budget or Excel-based AICP forms for bid creation
Presents recommended production company to client with cost justification

personas.name: Marcus Rivera — Line Producer
personas.role: Freelance Line Producer — 12 years in commercial production. Builds 6-8 AICP bid packages per month for production companies competing in triple bids.
personas.quote: I know every IATSE local's rate by heart because I've looked them up a thousand times. Half my value is just knowing what things cost.
goals: Hit target budget range while covering all production needs accurately
Populate 311+ line items with current union rates and equipment costs
Deliver complete bid package (topsheet + breakdown + letter) in 3 working days
Minimize markup calculation errors on high-stakes financial documents
painPoints: Scrolling through 300+ line items in Excel with no progressive disclosure
Manually looking up SAG-AFTRA / IATSE rates from external PDFs and websites
Maintaining separate client-facing topsheet and internal detailed breakdown
Multi-format deliverables — social, broadcast, digital each need separate bids
behaviors: Always starts with crew rates (Section B) — the largest variable cost
Builds out equipment, locations, art dept, talent, then post-production in order
Applies 25% production fee, 3% insurance, 15% travel handling at the end
Works with EP to adjust bid up or down to fit competitive budget range

personas.name: Diana Okafor — Cost Consultant
personas.role: Independent Cost Consultant — 15 years in production accounting. Hired by brands and agencies to audit bids, flag overcharges, and ensure AICP compliance.
personas.quote: Every production company formats their bid slightly differently. I'm comparing apples to oranges in three different spreadsheets.
goals: Identify inflated or understated line items across 3+ competing bids
Ensure bids comply with AICP standards and union rate minimums
Deliver clear, defensible comparison reports to client business affairs
Track bid-to-actual variance for post-production reconciliation
painPoints: No automated comparison tool — must review each bid line by line in Excel
Different bidders structure sections differently, breaking side-by-side alignment
Rate benchmarking relies on personal database built over 15 years
No standard diff to see what changed between bid revision v3 and v4
behaviors: Opens all 3+ bids simultaneously on dual monitors
Flags any line item variance above 15% threshold between competing bids
Cross-references SAG-AFTRA rate sheets and IATSE local agreements
Provides written analysis with recommend / flag / reject per section

Market Growth Reports — Ad Production Services
Busy Like / Vitrina AI — AI Production Budget Trends
Morgan Lewis / Akin Gump — Corporate Transparency Act
Ray Panko / Pak-Lok Poon — Spreadsheet Error Studies
Frame by Brand / Saturation.io — Tool Comparisons
Marketing Dive / Ad Age — DOJ Investigation Coverage
NNGroup / Pencil & Paper — UX & Progressive Disclosure
W3C WAI / AG Grid / MDN — Accessibility Standards
AICP Form Deep Analysis + Input Spec — Domain Expert
